title: Engineering electrically-switchable quantum anomalous Hall states by spin-orbit coupling
authors: Maosen Qin, Ziwei Wang, Gyeongmin Kim, Kenji Watanabe, Takashi Taniguchi, Steven H. Simon, Siddharth A. Parameswaran, Hryhoriy Polshyn
abstract: Nonvolatile gate-driven switching of quantum anomalous Hall (QAH) states in graphene moiré systems provides a promising route toward topological electronics based on chiral edge states. However, deliberate use of this switching mechanism requires control over both the magnetic properties and metastability of QAH states. While previous demonstrations mostly relied on the intrinsic magnetic energy landscape of moiré devices, here we show that this landscape can be engineered through proximity coupling to WSe2. We find that proximitizing twisted monolayer-bilayer graphene by WSe2 reshapes the magnetization reversals responsible for nonvolatile electrical switching of QAH states. We attribute this effect to the proximity-induced spin-orbit coupling (SOC), which can lock spin and valley and modify the magnetization of the competing states involved in switching compared with non-proximitized graphene systems. Our findings establish proximity-induced SOC as a new way to engineer magnetic properties and switchable magnetic states in graphene-based systems. We further demonstrate that strong magnetic metastability in tMBG allows the magnetic states to be gate-tuned between QAH and metallic regimes, and between QAH states with Chern numbers |C| = 2 and 1 without resetting the magnetic state. This functionality points toward new device architectures based on QAH chiral edge states.
summary: The research demonstrates that coupling twisted bilayer graphene with WSe2 induces strong Spin-Orbit Coupling (SOC), enabling the realization of robust, electrically switchable Quantum Anomalous Hall states. By controlling this SOC, researchers can engineer the magnetic state and tune the system between different topological regimes ($C=\pm 2$ to $C=\pm 1$) using gate voltages. This work establishes a powerful methodology for designing next-generation topological electronic devices.
main_problem: The research aims to engineer electrically-switchable Quantum Anomalous Hall (QAH) states in graphene moiré systems by controlling their magnetic properties.
main_result: Proximity coupling to WSe2 induces Spin-Orbit Coupling (SOC), which allows for the engineering of robust, nonvolatile QAH states and enables tuning between different Chern numbers without resetting the magnetic state.
method: The study combines transport measurements (measuring Hall/longitudinal resistance) with theoretical modeling using continuum Hamiltonians and self-consistent Hartree-Fock calculations to analyze spin and orbital magnetization.
model_or_system: The physical platform is twisted bilayer graphene (tMBG) interfaced with WSe2. The model incorporates the Bistritzer-MacDonald framework, adding proximity-induced Ising SOC ($\lambda_I$) and analyzing electron interactions via Hartree-Fock mean-field theory.
key_observables: Quantized Hall resistance ($R_{yx}$), longitudinal resistance ($R_{xx}$), magnetic hysteresis loops, Chern numbers ($C=\pm 2, \pm 1$), and orbital/spin magnetization components.
important_parameters:
Twist angle ($	heta \approx 1.1 - 1.5^\circ$), carrier density ($
u$), gate voltage displacement field ($D$), and the strength of the induced SOC ($\lambda_I$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that proximity-induced SOC dominates over intrinsic magnetic energy in certain regimes, allowing for controlled switching between spin/valley polarized ground states.
figures_summary:
Figures show quantized QAH plateaus ($R_{yx}$), gate-driven magnetic hysteresis loops demonstrating state switching, and theoretical plots illustrating the dependence of total magnetization on filling factor ($
u$) and SOC strength.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the physical platform (tMBG/WSe2) to confirming the key mechanism (SOC via quantum oscillations), demonstrating the primary effect (quantized QAH states and switching), and finally detailing the theoretical underpinning of magnetization control through spin-valley coupling.
why_interesting: This work provides a concrete example of how engineered proximity effects (SOC) can be used in 2D materials to stabilize topological phases and enable electrical control over fundamental quantum properties like chirality, which is highly relevant for designing novel spintronic/topological devices.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Quantized anomalous Hall (QAH) effect in tMBG/WSe2 device with θ = 1.23◦(D2). a, Schematic of a tMBG/WSe2 device consisting of rotationally misaligned monolayer graphene(MLG) and Bernal-stacked bilayer graphene(BLG) placed on a monolayer WSe2, encapsulated by hexagonal boron nitride (hBN). Top and bottom graphite gates control carrier density n and displacement field D. b, Calculated band structure of tMBG/WSe2 with proximity-induced Ising spin-orbit coupling of λI = 4 meV at D = 0.3, 0 and -0.5 V/nm. c, Longitudinal resistance Rxx measured in the conduction band at 50 mT and D > 0. Sharp dips in Rxx at ν = 1 and 3 mark QAH states surrounded by higher-resistance metallic isospin-...

FIG. 2. Magnetization reversal and nonvolatile electrical switching near ν = 1 QAH state in device D2. a,b, Ryx (a) and Rxx (b) as a function of n and D, measured at B= 0.5 T. The fast sweep axis is n, with the direction indicated by arrows. In addition to QAH state at ν = 1, a sharp change of the resistance at intermediate fillings near ν ≈1.2 −1.3 marks voltage-induced switching between magnetic states. c, Magnetic hysteresis of Ryx measured at D = 0.47 V/nm and n=1.40, 1.36, 1.32, 1.08, 0.96, 0.93, 0.89 ×1012cm−2, as indicated in a. Curves are offset and scaled for clarity, as indicated. d, Ryx measured as a function of B at D = 0.47 V/nm for opposite n sweep directions as indicated in...

FIG. 3. Gate-driven magnetic state switching in tMBG/WSe2 devices with higher twist angles. a, b, The difference between Ryx measured with two oppo- site n-sweep directions, ∆Ryx, in tMBG/WSe2 devices D4 (θ = 1.37◦) and D5 (θ = 1.47◦). Here, Ryx is antisymmetrized with respect to B. Device D4 is measured at D = 0.57 V/nm, whereas in D5, D spans 0.48 −0.63 V/nm, as indicated in Fig. E8. Solid and dashed hyperbolic lines near the MR tran- sitions are guides to the eye following B ∼1/(n−nrev), where nrev is the carrier density at which MR occurs.

FIG. 4. Gate-tunable Chern number in electrically switchable QAH states in tMBG. a, Ryx measured as a function of n and B in device D6, which does not contain WSe2(1.27◦), at D = 0.46 V/nm. The left and middle subpan- els show the resistance measured for different n-sweep direc- tions, while the right one plots the difference, ∆Ryx. Dashed lines originating from ν = 3 and 3.5 have slopes expected for Chern insulators with C = ±2 and C = ±1, respectively. b, QAH states at ν = 3 and ν = 3.5 in D6 evidenced by the quan- tized hysteresis of Ryx. c, Nonvolatile gate-driven switching of the magnetic state at B = 25 mT.
